subsections.title: 17. DESCRIPTIVE EPIDEMIOLOGY / TIME TRENDS
subsections.content:
Q1. Define epidemiology. Describe procedures involved in conducting descriptive
studies.
  - Year: 2021, 2018   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Epidemiology is the cornerstone of public health and preventive medicine. It provides a scientific framework
for understanding the distribution of diseases and the factors that influence them. The knowledge generated
by epidemiology guides the planning and evaluation of public health interventions and health care services.

DEFINITION OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

The most widely accepted definition is that of John M. Last (2001): "Epidemiology is the study of the
distribution and determinants of health-related states or events in specified populations, and the application
of this study to the control of health problems."
Distribution: refers to the frequency and pattern of health events in terms of Person, Place, and Time.
Determinants: refers to the causal factors — biological, physical, social, or environmental — that influence
the occurrence of disease.
Specified populations: emphasizes the population-based (not individual) nature of epidemiology.
Application: epidemiology is always goal-directed — to prevent and control disease.

SCOPE & AIMS OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

- To describe the distribution of disease in terms of Person, Place, and Time
- To identify the determinants and risk factors of disease
139

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of
Health
For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content
- To study the natural history and prognosis of disease
- To evaluate preventive and therapeutic measures
- To aid in health planning and policy formulation

TYPES OF EPIDEMIOLOGIC STUDIES

| Type | Sub-type | Key Feature | Descriptive | Cross-sectional, Case report, Case | series |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Describes disease distribution (Who, | Where, When) | Analytical | Case-control, Cohort | Tests hypotheses about causation | Experimental |


PROCEDURES IN CONDUCTING A DESCRIPTIVE STUDY

A descriptive study systematically collects and analyzes data to describe the occurrence of a disease or
health event in a population. The following steps are followed:
Step 1: Define the Problem
The health problem to be studied is clearly defined. This includes specifying the disease or health event,
identifying the affected population, and the time period of interest. Operational definitions of the case must
be established (e.g., clinical, laboratory, or epidemiological definitions).
Step 2: Review of Existing Literature
A comprehensive review of published literature, surveillance data, health records, vital statistics, and
hospital registers is done to understand what is already known about the disease. This helps identify gaps

| in knowledge and formulate research questions. | Step 3: Define the Study Population |
| --- | --- |
| The target population is clearly identified. Inclusion and exclusion criteria are established. Sampling | methods (random, systematic, stratified) are applied to select representative subjects to ensure |

disease registries, census data, and mortality statistics. Variables collected include:
- Person variables: Age, sex, race, ethnicity, socioeconomic status, occupation, marital status,
religion, lifestyle.
- Place variables: Country, region, urban/rural classification, geographic clusters, work environment.
- Time variables: Onset of illness, seasonal patterns, epidemic curves, secular trends.
Step 5: Data Organization & Analysis
Collected data are tabulated, classified, and analyzed using appropriate statistical methods. Rates and
ratios (incidence rate, prevalence rate, attack rate, mortality rate, case fatality rate) are calculated. Graphs,
maps, spot maps, and epidemic curves are constructed to visualize patterns.
Step 6: Interpretation & Hypothesis Generation
Patterns identified are interpreted in terms of Person, Place, and Time. From the observed patterns,
hypotheses about the causative factors are generated. Descriptive studies do NOT test hypotheses — they

| only generate them for further analytical investigation. | Step 7: Report and Communicate Findings | 140 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of | Health | For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content |

  Q2. Classify epidemiologic studies. Describe steps in conducting a cohort study.
  - Year: 2023   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Epidemiologic studies are systematic investigations designed to describe or analyze the distribution and
determinants of diseases in human populations. They are broadly classified into Observational Studies and
Experimental Studies.

CLASSIFICATION OF EPIDEMIOLOGIC STUDIES

I. Observational Studies
The investigator does not intervene; they merely observe events as they occur naturally.
A. Descriptive Studies: Describe the frequency and distribution of disease without testing any specific
hypothesis.
- Case Report: Detailed clinical description of a single patient with an unusual disease.
- Case Series: Description of a small group of patients with the same condition.
- Cross-Sectional (Prevalence) Study: Measures disease and exposure simultaneously at one point
in time. Gives Prevalence.
B. Analytical Studies: Test specific hypotheses about the relationship between exposure and disease.
- Case-Control Study: Begins with disease (cases) vs. no disease (controls); looks backward at
exposure. Gives Odds Ratio (OR). Best for RARE diseases.
- Cohort Study: Begins with exposure; follows forward for disease outcome. Gives Relative Risk

| (RR). Best for RARE exposures. | II. Experimental Studies |
| --- | --- |
| The investigator actively intervenes and controls the exposure or treatment. |  |

- Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT): Gold standard. Participants randomly assigned to treatment or
control group.
- Field Trial: Conducted in the community among healthy people at risk (e.g., vaccine trials).
- Community Trial: Entire communities assigned to interventions (e.g., fluoridation of water).

| Feature | Case-Control | Cohort | RCT | Direction | Backward |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| (Retrospective) | Forward (Prospective) | Forward | Starting point | Disease status | Exposure status |


COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of

| Health | For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content |
| --- | --- |
| | Feature | Case-Control | Cohort | RCT | Time | Short | | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | |
| COHORT STUDY — DEFINITION |  |
| A cohort study is an observational study in which a defined group of people (a cohort) who share a common | characteristic or experience (the exposure) are followed over time to determine the rate at which disease |
| STEPS IN CONDUCTING A COHORT STUDY |  |
| Step 1: Selection of the Cohorts | Two groups are identified at the outset: the Exposed Cohort (individuals with the risk factor or exposure, |

Detailed information is recorded at baseline including: nature and degree of exposure, demographic details
(age, sex, occupation, socioeconomic status), potential confounding variables, and current health status.
Standardized methods (questionnaires, biological markers, environmental measurements) are used to

| minimize information bias. | Step 3: Follow-up of Both Cohorts |
| --- | --- |
| Both exposed and unexposed groups are followed over a defined period (months to decades depending | on the disease latency period). Regular contact is maintained through periodic examinations, |

The following measures are calculated from a 2x2 contingency table:
- Incidence in Exposed (Ie) = a / (a + b)
- Incidence in Unexposed (Iue) = c / (c + d)
- Relative Risk (RR) = Ie / Iue  — Measures strength of association
- Attributable Risk (AR) = Ie − Iue  — Measures excess risk due to exposure
- Population Attributable Risk (PAR) = Incidence in total population − Iue
- Attributable Risk Percent (AR%) = (AR / Ie) x 100
Step 6: Interpretation & Conclusion
If RR > 1: Exposure is a risk factor. If RR < 1: Exposure is protective. If RR = 1: No association. The
magnitude of RR reflects the strength of the association. Dose-response relationship, consistency, and

| biological plausibility are assessed to determine causality. | 142 |
| --- | --- |
| COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of | Health |

ADVANTAGES & DISADVANTAGES

| Advantages | Disadvantages | Can directly calculate Incidence Rate | Very expensive and time-consuming | Temporal sequence established (cause before | effect) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Requires large sample size | Can study multiple outcomes of one exposure | Loss to follow-up is a major bias | Best for studying rare exposures | Not suitable for rare diseases | No recall bias (prospective) |


EXAM TIP: Cohort = RR. Case-Control = OR. When RR and OR are quoted together, note that OR
approximates RR only when the disease is rare (rare disease assumption).
CONCLUSION: Cohort studies provide the strongest observational evidence for causation by establishing
temporal sequence. While costly and time-consuming, they are indispensable for studying exposures with
long latency periods and for calculating true incidence rates.

  Q3. How do you classify Descriptive Epidemiology? Describe TIME TRENDS.
  - Year: 2011   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Descriptive epidemiology systematically characterizes the occurrence and distribution of diseases in a
population. The three pillars of descriptive epidemiology are Person, Place, and Time — collectively forming
the epidemiological triad of distribution. Time trends are one of the most informative dimensions of disease
distribution.

CLASSIFICATION OF DESCRIPTIVE EPIDEMIOLOGY

I. By Person
Person characteristics define who is affected by the disease. These include:
- Age: The most important determinant; most diseases show age-specific incidence patterns.
- Sex/Gender: Biological differences (hormones, immunity) and sociocultural roles influence disease
patterns. E.g., coronary artery disease is more common in males.
- Race and Ethnicity: Influence both genetic susceptibility and socioeconomic exposure.
- Socioeconomic Status (SES): Income, education, and occupation determine exposure to risk
factors and access to healthcare.
- Occupation: Determines specific environmental and chemical exposures. E.g., silicosis in miners.
- Marital Status & Religion: Influence lifestyle, reproductive patterns, and dietary habits.
- Lifestyle habits: Smoking, alcohol use, diet, physical activity.
II. By Place
Place characteristics identify geographic variation in disease occurrence:
- International variation: Comparing disease rates across countries reveals the role of genetic,
cultural, and environmental factors.
- National/Regional variation: Disease burden differs between states or regions.
143

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of
Health
For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content
- Urban vs Rural: Urban areas have higher rates of NCDs; rural areas may have higher
communicable disease burden.
- Local clustering: Spot maps help identify disease clusters around a common source (e.g., water
supply).
- Migrant studies: Migrants who adopt the disease pattern of the host country confirm environmental

| over genetic causation. | III. By Time |
| --- | --- |
| Time trends reveal how the disease frequency changes over different time scales. This is described in | detail below. |
| TIME TRENDS IN EPIDEMIOLOGY |  |
| Time trends (temporal variations) refer to patterns of disease occurrence over time. They are crucial for | generating hypotheses about disease causation, identifying risk factors, evaluating the impact of public |

| Time trends are classified into three major types: | 1. Short-Term Fluctuations (Epidemic Variations) |
| --- | --- |
| These represent sudden increases in disease frequency over a short period. They are best represented by | an EPIDEMIC CURVE (number of cases plotted against time of onset). |

These are predictable fluctuations in disease occurrence over regular intervals:
a) Seasonal Variation: Disease incidence increases predictably during certain seasons due to
environmental changes affecting the agent, host, or vector. Examples: Respiratory infections (winter
months — dry cold air, indoor crowding); Malaria and dengue (monsoon — breeding of mosquitoes);
Diarrheal diseases (summer — food spoilage); Meningococcal meningitis (dry, dusty season).
b) Cyclic (Inter-annual) Variation: Some diseases recur in cycles of several years, related to build-up of
susceptible individuals in the population after previous outbreaks. Examples: Influenza pandemics
(approximately every 10–15 years); Measles (every 2–3 years in unvaccinated populations).
3. Secular (Long-Term) Trends
Secular trends are gradual changes in disease frequency over a period of decades. They reflect the
cumulative effect of changes in lifestyle, environment, medical advances, socioeconomic conditions, and
public health programs.
Examples of rising secular trends: Cardiovascular diseases, type 2 diabetes, obesity, mental health
disorders — due to sedentary lifestyles, urbanization, and dietary changes.
Examples of declining secular trends: Tuberculosis (decades before antibiotics — due to improved
nutrition and living standards); Poliomyelitis (vaccination programs); Smallpox (eradication by 1980).
144

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of

| Health | For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content |
| --- | --- |
| Secular trends are extremely valuable for: Evaluating the long-term effectiveness of vaccination programs | and health policies; Planning and allocation of health resources; Understanding the epidemiological |
| | Type | Time Scale | Curve Shape | Examples | Point Source Epidemic | Hours to days | | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | |
| EXAM TIP: Mnemonics for Time Trends: SPACE = Short-term (epidemic), Periodic (cyclic), And secular | (long-term) Cyclical Events. Epidemic curves are the most common MCQ topic on Time Trends. |

  Q4. Discuss Relative Risk vs Attributable Risk.
  - Year: 2018   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Relative Risk (RR) and Attributable Risk (AR) are two fundamental measures of disease association
derived from cohort studies. While both are calculated from the same 2x2 contingency table, they serve
different purposes. RR measures the STRENGTH of an association (etiological significance), while AR
measures the PUBLIC HEALTH IMPACT of an exposure (preventive significance).
THE 2x2 CONTINGENCY TABLE

| Disease (+) | Disease (−) | Total | Exposed (+) | a | b |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| a + b | Unexposed (−) | c | d | c + d | Total |


| Incidence in Exposed (Ie): a / (a + b) | Incidence in Unexposed (Iue): c / (c + d) |
| --- | --- |
| RELATIVE RISK (RR) |  |
| Definition | 145 |
| COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of | Health |

- RR = 1: No association between exposure and disease.
- RR > 1: Positive association — exposure INCREASES the risk of disease (risk factor). Example:
RR of 10 for smoking and lung cancer means smokers are 10 times more likely to develop lung
cancer.
- RR < 1: Negative association — exposure DECREASES the risk of disease (protective factor).
Example: RR of 0.3 for exercise and heart disease means physically active people have 70% lower
risk.
Significance of RR
- Measures the STRENGTH of association between exposure and disease.
- Essential for establishing causation (Bradford Hill criteria — strength of association).
- Used to identify etiological (causative) agents.
- Applied in clinical risk assessment and evidence-based medicine.
- RR is not calculated in case-control studies (Odds Ratio is used instead).

ATTRIBUTABLE RISK (AR)

Definition
Attributable Risk (also called Risk Difference or Excess Risk) is the absolute difference in the incidence of
disease between the exposed and unexposed groups. It represents the portion of disease incidence in the
exposed group that is attributable to the exposure — the excess risk due solely to the exposure.

| Formula: AR = Ie − Iue = [a/(a+b)] − [c/(c+d)] | Interpretation of AR |
| --- | --- |
| AR quantifies the actual excess number of cases per unit population that can be attributed to the exposure. | Example: If Ie = 70 per 1000 and Iue = 10 per 1000, then AR = 60 per 1000 — meaning 60 extra cases per |
| COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of | Health |
| | Feature | Relative Risk (RR) | Attributable Risk (AR) | Study used in | Cohort studies | Cohort studies | | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | |
| PRACTICAL EXAMPLE |  |

A cohort study on smoking (exposed) vs non-smoking (unexposed) and lung cancer:
- Incidence in smokers (Ie) = 70 per 1,000
- Incidence in non-smokers (Iue) = 10 per 1,000
- RR = 70/10 = 7 — Smokers are 7 times more likely to develop lung cancer
- AR = 70 − 10 = 60 per 1,000 — 60 extra cases per 1,000 are due to smoking
- AR% = (60/70) x 100 = 85.7% — 85.7% of lung cancer in smokers is attributable to smoking
EXAM TIP: RR = Strength of causation (important for the researcher identifying causes). AR = Public health
importance (important for the policy maker planning prevention). Both are derived from cohort studies. OR
(Odds Ratio) is the case-control equivalent of RR.
CONCLUSION: RR and AR are complementary measures. A high RR identifies a strong causal link (e.g.,
smoking and lung cancer). A high AR identifies high-priority targets for prevention programs (e.g., how
many deaths can be prevented by smoking cessation). Together, they provide a comprehensive picture of
disease causation and prevention potential.

subsections.title: 18. DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH
subsections.content:
Q1. Enumerate determinants of health. Discuss behavioral determinants for prevention of

COVID-19.

  - Year: 2020   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being — not merely the absence of disease
(WHO, 1948). The level of health in any community is determined by a complex interplay of multiple factors
called the determinants of health. Understanding these determinants is essential for designing effective
preventive and promotive health interventions.

DEFINITION OF HEALTH DETERMINANTS

"Determinants of health are a range of personal, social, economic, and environmental factors that
determine the health status of individuals or populations." — WHO
147

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of

| Health | For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content |
| --- | --- |
| ENUMERATION OF DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH |  |
| Health determinants are best understood through the Lalonde Model (1974), which categorizes them into |  |

| four broad fields: | 1. Biological and Genetic Factors |
| --- | --- |
| These are intrinsic, largely non-modifiable host factors that influence susceptibility and resistance to | disease. |

- Age: Disease patterns change throughout life — infections predominate in childhood; NCDs in
middle age and old age.
- Sex: Biological differences (hormonal, immunological) influence disease susceptibility. E.g., women
have stronger immune responses; men have higher cardiovascular risk before menopause.
- Genetic constitution: Inherited disorders (sickle cell anaemia, thalassemia, hemophilia), genetic
predisposition to cancer, diabetes, hypertension.
- Immune status: Natural immunity, vaccination status, immune deficiencies influence disease

| occurrence. | 2. Behavioral / Lifestyle Factors |
| --- | --- |
| These are modifiable determinants related to individual behaviors and choices. They are increasingly | important given the global rise of non-communicable diseases (NCDs). |

- Smoking: Leading cause of preventable death globally — lung cancer, COPD, cardiovascular
disease.
- Diet and Nutrition: High-fat, high-sugar diets → obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease.
Malnutrition → stunting, micronutrient deficiencies.
- Physical activity: Sedentary lifestyle → obesity, hypertension, depression. Regular exercise is
protective against NCDs.
- Alcohol and substance use: Liver cirrhosis, accidents, mental illness, fetal alcohol syndrome.
- Sexual behavior: Unsafe sex → STIs including HIV/AIDS.
- Sleep patterns: Inadequate sleep → metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, mental health
problems.
- Health-seeking behavior: Delayed care-seeking, non-adherence to treatment worsens health

| outcomes. | 3. Environmental Factors |
| --- | --- |
| Physical Environment: Air quality (air pollution → respiratory disease), water quality (contamination → | diarrheal disease), sanitation, climate change, housing quality, occupational exposures (chemicals, dust, |

- Availability: Presence of hospitals, clinics, trained health workers.
- Accessibility: Geographic, financial, cultural accessibility of services.
- Quality: Adequacy of diagnosis, treatment, referral systems.
- Preventive services: Immunization programs, screening, antenatal care.
5. Socioeconomic and Political Factors
- Education: Higher education → better health literacy, healthier behaviors, better employment.
- Income and poverty: Poverty is the single greatest determinant of poor health globally.
148

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of
Health
For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content
- Employment: Unemployment → stress, poverty, poor nutrition. Hazardous occupations →
occupational diseases.
- Governance and policy: Health legislation, welfare policies, social security systems.
- Globalization: Trade, migration, and cultural exchange create new health challenges.

BEHAVIORAL DETERMINANTS FOR PREVENTION OF COVID-19

COVID-19 (caused by SARS-CoV-2) was declared a global pandemic by WHO in March 2020. The virus
spreads primarily via respiratory droplets, aerosols, and contact with contaminated surfaces. Since specific
antiviral treatment was initially unavailable, behavioral modification became the PRIMARY intervention for
disease control. The following behavioral measures were essential:
1. Hand Hygiene
Frequent handwashing with soap and water for at least 20 seconds effectively inactivates the virus. Alcohol-
based hand sanitizers (minimum 60% alcohol) were used when soap was unavailable. People were
instructed to wash hands: after coughing/sneezing, before touching face, after contact with surfaces, before
and after eating, after using toilet, and after coming from public spaces.
2. Respiratory Etiquette
Covering the mouth and nose with the bent elbow or a tissue while coughing or sneezing prevents droplet
spread. Used tissues were to be discarded immediately in closed bins and hands washed subsequently.

| Spitting in public places was prohibited. | 3. Face Masking |
| --- | --- |
| Wearing a well-fitting mask (N95 respirator, 3-ply surgical mask, or cloth mask in resource-limited settings) | significantly reduces transmission both from and to the wearer. Masks were mandatory in healthcare |

Quarantine: Contacts of confirmed cases quarantined for 14 days (the maximum incubation period) to

| monitor for symptoms. | 8. Vaccination |
| --- | --- |
| COVID-19 vaccines (Covishield, Covaxin, Pfizer-BioNTech, Moderna, Sputnik V, Johnson & Johnson) were | deployed globally. Vaccination significantly reduced severe disease, hospitalization, ICU admission, and |
| COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of | Health |

- Adequate nutrition to support immune function.
- Regular physical activity (home-based exercises during lockdowns).
- Stress management and mental health support (pandemic-related anxiety, depression).
- Adequate sleep (7-9 hours) to maintain immune function.
- Avoiding alcohol and tobacco which impair immune response.
EXAM TIP: Remember the COVID prevention framework: HANDS (hygiene) + FACE (mask + no face
touching) + SPACE (distancing) + VENTILATION + VACCINATION. This 5-point framework covers all
behavioral determinants comprehensively.
CONCLUSION: Behavioral determinants were the frontline of defense against COVID-19. A combination of
hand hygiene, masking, distancing, ventilation, isolation, and vaccination created multiple barriers to
transmission (Swiss Cheese Model). These non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs), though simple, were
proven to be highly effective in flattening the epidemic curve and reducing mortality.

  Q2. What are the determinants of health?
  - Year: 2022   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

The determinants of health are the broad range of factors that influence the health of individuals and
communities. Health is not determined by biology or healthcare alone; it is shaped by a complex web of
interacting factors. Understanding determinants is essential for addressing root causes of ill-health and
promoting health equity.

MODELS OF HEALTH DETERMINANTS

1. Lalonde Model (1974) — Health Field Concept
Marc Lalonde, Canadian Minister of Health, proposed four determinants of health: Human Biology,
Environment, Lifestyle/Behavior, and Health Care Organization. This model established that most
premature deaths are caused by lifestyle and environmental factors, not by biology or lack of healthcare.
2. Dahlgren and Whitehead's Rainbow Model (1991)
The most widely cited and examined model. It depicts health determinants as concentric layers around the
individual, like a rainbow:
- Core (fixed): Age, sex, and hereditary factors — non-modifiable.
- Layer 1 — Individual lifestyle factors: Smoking, diet, physical activity, alcohol. These are modifiable
behaviors.
- Layer 2 — Social and community networks: Family, friends, social cohesion, social support
systems.
- Layer 3 — Living and working conditions: Education, employment, food security, housing,

| healthcare services, work environment, water and sanitation. | 150 |
| --- | --- |
| COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of | Health |

- Layer 4 (outermost) — General socioeconomic, cultural, and environmental conditions: Economic
policies, social inequality, cultural norms, environmental quality.
The model emphasizes that the outer layers (socioeconomic conditions) shape the inner layers (individual
behaviors), making health a social issue, not just an individual responsibility.

DETAILED ENUMERATION OF HEALTH DETERMINANTS

A. Biological and Genetic Factors
- Age and sex: Determine baseline disease susceptibility.
- Genetic factors: Hereditary diseases (e.g., thalassemia, PKU), familial predisposition to NCDs.
- Immune status: Determines susceptibility to infections.
- Physiological changes: Pregnancy, puberty, menopause — alter health needs and risks.
B. Behavioral / Lifestyle Factors
- Tobacco use: Causes lung cancer, COPD, cardiovascular disease, stroke.
- Diet: Malnutrition causes deficiency diseases; over-nutrition causes obesity and metabolic
syndrome.
- Physical activity: Sedentary lifestyle is associated with NCDs.
- Alcohol and drug abuse: Cause liver disease, accidents, mental illness.
- Sexual behavior: Risk factor for STIs including HIV.
C. Physical Environment
- Air quality: Indoor and outdoor air pollution causes respiratory and cardiovascular disease.
- Water and sanitation: Contaminated water causes cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A.
- Climate: Temperature extremes cause heat stroke and hypothermia. Climate change is increasing
vector-borne disease range.
- Housing: Overcrowding facilitates spread of TB, scabies, respiratory infections.
D. Social Environment
- Social support networks: Strong social ties are protective for mental and physical health.
- Social norms and culture: Shape behaviors around food, health-seeking, substance use.
- Social exclusion and discrimination: Marginalized groups have worse health outcomes.

  Q3. What are the social determinants of health?
  - Year: 2023   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Social determinants of health (SDOH) are defined by WHO as: "The conditions in which people are born,
grow, live, work, and age, including the health system. These circumstances are shaped by the distribution
of money, power, and resources at global, national, and local levels." (WHO Commission on Social
Determinants of Health, 2008)
SDOH are responsible for the majority of health inequities observed within and between countries. They
are the primary drivers of preventable illness, disability, and premature death globally. Unlike biological
determinants, SDOHs are modifiable through policy action.

KEY SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH

1. Income and Social Protection
Income is the most fundamental social determinant. Low income limits access to adequate nutrition, safe
housing, health care, and education. Income inequality (the gap between rich and poor) is associated with
worse population health outcomes even in high-income countries. Social protection programs
(unemployment benefits, pensions, conditional cash transfers, disability allowances) buffer against the
health risks of income insecurity. Example: Countries with strong social safety nets (Scandinavian
countries) consistently report better health outcomes and lower infant mortality.
2. Education and Literacy
Education is one of the most powerful determinants of health. It operates through multiple pathways: higher
education leads to better employment and income, greater health literacy (ability to understand and act on
health information), healthier behaviors (less smoking, better diet, more exercise), and greater
empowerment to navigate health systems. Low literacy leads to inability to read prescriptions, difficulty
understanding health messages, and poor treatment adherence. Female education is particularly important

19. STATISTICAL TESTS OF SIGNIFICANCE

Q1. Enumerate Statistical Tests of Significance. How are they useful in Research
Methodology? (2022, 2013, 2012) ★★★★★

INTRODUCTION

Statistical tests of significance are mathematical procedures used to determine whether an observed
difference or association between groups is statistically significant or merely due to chance. They form
the cornerstone of inferential statistics in biomedical research.

FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPTS

Null Hypothesis (H₀):
States there is no real difference or association. Example: H₀: Drug A and Drug B have equal efficacy.
Alternative Hypothesis (H₁/Ha):
States there is a real difference. Can be one-tailed or two-tailed.
p-value:
Probability of obtaining observed results (or more extreme) if H₀ is true. p < 0.05 → statistically significant
(5% level). p < 0.01 → highly significant (1% level). p < 0.001 → very highly significant.
Type I Error (α): Rejecting H₀ when it is actually true (false positive). Conventional α = 0.05.
Type II Error (β): Accepting H₀ when it is actually false (false negative). Power = 1-β.
Confidence Interval (CI): Range of values within which true population parameter lies with specified
confidence (usually 95%).

CLASSIFICATION OF TESTS OF SIGNIFICANCE

A. PARAMETRIC TESTS (require normal distribution assumption)

| Test | Use | Data Type | Student's t-test (unpaired) | Compare means of 2 independent groups | Continuous, normal data |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Student's t-test (paired) | Compare | means | in | same | group |


ANOVA

| alternative | Spearman's rank correlation (ρ) | Correlation — ordinal data | Rank/ordinal | 161 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Fisher's exact test | Small sample categorical data | Small expected frequencies | McNemar's test | Paired categorical data |
| C. TESTS FOR SURVIVAL DATA |  |  |  |  |

- Log-rank test: Compare survival curves between groups
- Kaplan-Meier estimator: Estimate survival function
- Cox proportional hazards regression: Multivariate survival analysis

USEFULNESS IN RESEARCH METHODOLOGY

1. HYPOTHESIS TESTING
- Provides objective criteria to accept or reject null hypothesis
- Converts clinical questions into testable statistical hypotheses
- Standardizes decision-making across researchers globally
2. SAMPLE SIZE CALCULATION
- Statistical tests determine required sample size for adequate power (usually 80-90%)
- Prevents underpowered studies (Type II error) and overpowered studies (waste of resources)
3. COMPARISON OF INTERVENTIONS
- RCTs use t-tests, ANOVA to compare treatment groups
- Chi-square tests compare proportions (e.g., cure rates, event rates)
4. EXPLORATION OF ASSOCIATIONS
- Correlation coefficients quantify strength of relationships
- Regression models identify independent predictors while controlling confounders
5. VALIDITY OF DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
- Sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV calculated and tested statistically
- ROC curves and area under curve (AUC) with CIs
6. META-ANALYSIS
- Pooled effect sizes with CIs across multiple studies
- Heterogeneity tested by I² statistic and Q-test
7. INTERPRETATION OF CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE vs STATISTICAL SIGNIFICANCE
- Statistical significance ≠ clinical significance
- Large samples can make trivial differences statistically significant
- Confidence intervals provide clinical context to p-values
EXAM TIP: This is a 5-star repeat question. Always: (1) Define null hypothesis, p-value, errors; (2) Give
classified table of tests; (3) Discuss uses in research. Mentioning 'parametric vs non-parametric' choice
criteria earns bonus marks.
Q2. Discuss the role of Chi-square test in Biomedical Research (2012) ★★★★

INTRODUCTION

The Chi-square (χ²) test, developed by Karl Pearson (1900), is the most commonly used non-parametric
test in biomedical research for analyzing categorical data. It tests whether observed frequencies differ
significantly from expected frequencies.

TYPES OF CHI-SQUARE TEST

| Type | Use | Chi-square test of independence | Tests association between 2 categorical variables | (contingency table analysis) | 162 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| | Chi-square goodness-of-fit test | Tests whether observed frequency distribution fits | an expected distribution | McNemar's Chi-square test | | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | | Tests paired categorical data (before-after studies) | Mantel-Haenszel Chi-square | Tests association controlling for confounding | variable (stratified analysis) | |  |  |  |
| FORMULA |  |  |  |  |  |
| χ² = Σ [(O - E)² / E] | Where O = Observed frequency, E = Expected frequency | E = (Row total × Column total) / Grand total | Degrees of freedom (df) = (r-1)(c-1) where r = rows, c = columns |  |  |
| CONDITIONS FOR USE |  |  |  |  |  |

- Data should be in frequencies (counts), not percentages
- Expected frequency in each cell should be ≥5 (if not, use Fisher's exact test)
- Observations should be independent
- Total sample size should be ≥20

PROCEDURE — 2×2 CONTINGENCY TABLE

| Step 1: Set up 2×2 or larger contingency table | Step 2: Calculate expected frequencies for each cell | Step 3: Calculate χ² = Σ[(O-E)²/E] | Step 4: Determine degrees of freedom |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Step 5: Compare calculated χ² with table value at appropriate df and significance level | Step 6: If calculated χ² > table value → reject H₀ → statistically significant association |  |  |
| YATES' CORRECTION FOR CONTINUITY |  |  |  |

Applied for 2×2 tables when any expected cell frequency is between 5-10:
χ²(corrected) = Σ [(|O - E| - 0.5)² / E]
Reduces the χ² value, making the test more conservative and reducing Type I error.

APPLICATIONS IN BIOMEDICAL RESEARCH

- Comparing disease frequency between exposed and unexposed groups (cohort studies)
- Comparing exposure rates between cases and controls (case-control studies)
- Evaluating vaccine/drug efficacy by comparing outcome proportions
- Testing association between risk factors and disease (e.g., smoking and lung cancer)
- Comparing treatment adherence rates across demographic groups
- Genetic studies: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium testing
- Diagnostic test evaluation: comparing test results with gold standard

LIMITATIONS

- Cannot determine direction or strength of association (use Cramer's V for strength)
- Cannot be used for continuous data
- Cannot establish causality
- Not suitable for small samples (n<20) or expected cell frequency <5
- Does not indicate which specific category pairs differ (post-hoc tests needed)
EXAM TIP: For Chi-square questions: Always give the formula, Yates' correction, conditions for use,
and at least 5 applications. Fisher's exact test is the alternative for small samples — always mention it.

Q3. Discuss Different Steps in Hypothesis Testing (2020) ★★★

DEFINITION

Hypothesis testing is a formal statistical procedure to make inferences about a population parameter
based on sample data. It provides a systematic framework for deciding whether the evidence from data
supports or refutes a specific claim about the population.

STEPS IN HYPOTHESIS TESTING

STEP 1: STATE THE HYPOTHESES

- Null Hypothesis (H₀): No difference/association exists (status quo)
- Alternative Hypothesis (H₁): A real difference/association exists
- Two-tailed test: H₁ states difference in either direction
- One-tailed test: H₁ states difference in specific direction
Example: H₀: Mean SBP in intervention group = Mean SBP in control group; H₁: Mean SBP differs
between groups
STEP 2: SET THE SIGNIFICANCE LEVEL (α)
- Pre-determined probability of Type I error
- Conventional levels: α = 0.05 (5%), 0.01 (1%), 0.001 (0.1%)
- Medical research usually uses α = 0.05
- For multiple comparisons, use Bonferroni correction: α/k where k = number of tests

STEP 3: SELECT THE APPROPRIATE STATISTICAL TEST

- Based on: type of data (nominal/ordinal/continuous), number of groups, independent vs paired,
distribution assumption
- Parametric: t-test, ANOVA, correlation for normally distributed continuous data
- Non-parametric: Chi-square, Mann-Whitney for categorical/ordinal/skewed data

STEP 4: COLLECT DATA AND COMPUTE TEST STATISTIC

- Compute the appropriate test statistic (t, F, χ², z, U)
- This converts the observed data into a standardized value
STEP 5: DETERMINE THE p-VALUE OR CRITICAL VALUE
- Compare computed statistic with theoretical distribution (t, F, χ² tables)
- Determine the p-value: probability of getting the observed result if H₀ is true
- If p < α → reject H₀; if p ≥ α → fail to reject H₀

STEP 6: MAKE A STATISTICAL DECISION

| Decision Criterion | Interpretation | p < α (e.g., p < 0.05) | Reject H₀ — Result is statistically significant | p ≥ α (e.g., p ≥ 0.05) | Fail to reject H₀ — Result is not statistically | significant |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| p < 0.01 | Highly significant | p < 0.001 | Very highly significant |  |  |  |
| STEP 7: DRAW A CONCLUSION AND INTERPRET IN CONTEXT |  |  |  |  |  |  |

- Translate statistical decision into clinical/biological terms
- Report effect size and confidence intervals alongside p-value
- Acknowledge limitations and potential for error
- Statistical significance ≠ clinical significance — always interpret magnitude

ERRORS IN HYPOTHESIS TESTING

| Concept | Definition | Type I (α) Error | Reject true H₀ — False positive — Finding | significance when there is none | Type II (β) Error | Accept false H₀ — False negative — Missing real |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| significance | Power (1-β) | Probability of correctly rejecting false H₀ — Aim for |  |  |  |  |
| ≥80% |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Confidence Interval |  |  |  |  |  |  |

Quantifies uncertainty — more informative than p-
value alone

p-value → Decision → Conclusion. Always mention Type I/II errors, power, and CI.

Q1 & Q2. Describe / Discuss Eugenics and Euthenics & Their Role in Control of
Genetic Diseases (2011, 2013) ★★★★★

EUGENICS — DEFINITION

Eugenics (Greek: 'eu' = good; 'genos' = race/birth) is the science of improving a human population by
controlled breeding to increase the occurrence of desirable heritable characteristics. The term was coined
by Sir Francis Galton in 1883.

TYPES OF EUGENICS

A. POSITIVE EUGENICS

Encouraging reproduction among individuals with desirable genetic traits to improve the genetic quality
of the population.
- Incentives for marriage and reproduction among genetically 'fit' individuals
- Education and genetic counseling to promote informed reproductive choices
- Encouraging academically/intellectually gifted individuals to have more children

B. NEGATIVE EUGENICS

Discouraging or preventing reproduction among individuals with undesirable genetic traits.
- Voluntary or involuntary sterilization of individuals with hereditary disorders
- Marriage restrictions for those with genetic disorders
- Segregation in institutions
NOTE: Negative eugenics led to horrific abuses under Nazi Germany (eugenics programs, racial hygiene
laws). It is widely condemned today as pseudoscientific, unethical, and a human rights violation.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT AND ETHICAL ISSUES

- Galton's eugenics was misused to justify racism, colonialism, and genocide
- Forced sterilization programs in USA (Buck v Bell, 1927), Nazi Germany
- Modern genetics has discredited simplistic eugenics — most traits are polygenic and
environmentally influenced
- Modern medical ethics: autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence oppose coercive eugenics
- Contemporary 'liberal eugenics' — individual choice in reproductive decisions — remains
controversial

ROLE IN CONTROL OF GENETIC DISEASES

- Genetic screening programs identify carriers and affected individuals
- Prenatal diagnosis (amniocentesis, CVS) enables informed reproductive decisions
- Voluntary genetic counseling empowers families without coercion
- Population-based screening for high-frequency disorders (e.g., thalassaemia screening in Cyprus
— 97% reduction in thalassaemic births through voluntary programs)

EUTHENICS — DEFINITION

Euthenics (Greek: 'eu' = good; 'thenos' = strength) is the science of improving human beings by improving
their environment. It was developed as a counter to eugenics, emphasizing that nurture (environment) is
as important as nature (genes) in determining human health and potential.
167

The term is associated with Ellen H. Richards (1910) who emphasized that environmental improvement
— nutrition, sanitation, housing, education — can optimize human development regardless of genetic
endowment.

COMPONENTS OF EUTHENICS

1. NUTRITIONAL ENVIRONMENT
- Adequate nutrition in early life (first 1000 days) prevents stunting, optimizes brain development
- Micronutrient supplementation (iron, folic acid, iodine) prevents neural tube defects, cretinism
- School feeding programs improve cognitive performance
2. PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT
- Clean water, sanitation (WASH) prevents diarrhoeal diseases and stunting
- Clean air prevents respiratory diseases
- Safe housing prevents injuries and reduces communicable disease transmission
3. SOCIAL ENVIRONMENT
- Education — especially female literacy — is the most powerful euthenicist tool
- Social protection programs reduce poverty-induced deprivation
- Maternal and child health services
4. PSYCHOSOCIAL ENVIRONMENT
- Nurturing caregiving environments optimize neurodevelopment
- Early childhood development programs (ECCE)

Q3. Genetic Counseling — Discuss in Detail (2012) ★★★
168

DEFINITION

Genetic counseling is the process of helping people understand and adapt to the medical, psychological,
and familial implications of genetic contributions to disease. It involves communication of genetic
information, risk assessment, and provision of support to enable informed decision-making. (ASHG
definition, updated 2006)

OBJECTIVES

| 12. Establish accurate diagnosis of genetic disorder | 13. Estimate recurrence risks for family members |
| --- | --- |
| 14. Explain available reproductive options and their implications | 15. Provide psychological support and facilitate coping |
| WHO NEEDS GENETIC COUNSELING? |  |

- Couples with family history of genetic disorders (haemophilia, cystic fibrosis, thalassaemia)
- Consanguineous marriages (autosomal recessive risks doubled)
- Advanced maternal age ≥35 years (Down syndrome risk: 1 in 350 at 35, 1 in 100 at 40)
- Previous child with chromosomal anomaly or congenital malformation
- Recurrent miscarriages (≥3) — possible chromosomal translocation
- Couples who are known carriers of genetic conditions
- Exposure to teratogens (radiation, drugs, infections) during pregnancy
- Abnormal prenatal screening results (triple/quadruple test, NIPT)

PROCESS OF GENETIC COUNSELING

Step 1: DIAGNOSIS
- Detailed personal and family history — minimum 3-generation pedigree
- Physical examination, chromosomal analysis (karyotype), molecular genetic tests
- Biochemical tests (enzyme assays for inborn errors of metabolism)
Step 2: RISK ASSESSMENT
- Calculate recurrence risk based on pattern of inheritance

| Inheritance Pattern | Recurrence Risk | Autosomal Dominant | 50% risk for each child | Autosomal Recessive | 25% risk if both parents are carriers |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| X-linked Recessive | 50% of sons affected; 50% of daughters are | carriers | Chromosomal (trisomy) | 1-2% recurrence risk above age-specific baseline | Multifactorial |

- Non-directive counseling — present options without imposing views
- Use simple language, visual aids (pedigree diagrams)
- Allow questions and ensure comprehension
- Written summary of discussion provided
Step 4: DISCUSSION OF OPTIONS
- Prenatal diagnosis: Amniocentesis (16-20 weeks), Chorionic Villus Sampling — CVS (10-13
weeks), NIPT (Non-invasive prenatal testing) from 10 weeks
- Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT) with IVF
- Accepting risk and proceeding with pregnancy
169

- Adoption or use of donor gametes
- Refraining from reproduction
Step 5: PSYCHOLOGICAL SUPPORT
- Address guilt, anxiety, denial, grief
- Refer to support groups and mental health services
- Follow-up counseling sessions

PRINCIPLES OF GENETIC COUNSELING

- Non-directiveness: Counselor facilitates, does not direct reproductive choices
- Confidentiality: Results not disclosed without consent
- Autonomy: Respect for individual and family decision-making
- Informed consent for all genetic testing
- Beneficence and non-maleficence

GENETIC COUNSELING IN INDIA

- Integrated into National Health Mission (NHM)
- Available at medical colleges, AIIMS, regional genetic centers
- National Institute of Biomedical Genomics (NIBMG) supports genetic research
- Integrated Genetic and Rare Disease Portal
- PCPNDT Act regulates prenatal sex determination to prevent misuse
EXAM TIP: Genetic counseling is a potential long essay. Cover: Definition → Who needs it → Process (5
steps) → Principles (non-directive key term) → India context. Recurrence risk table is very high scoring.
Q4. Human Genome Project — Discuss (2013, 2020) ★★★

INTRODUCTION

The Human Genome Project (HGP) was an international scientific research project with the primary goal
of determining the sequence of nucleotide base pairs that make up human DNA and identifying and
mapping all of the genes of the human genome from both a physical and a functional standpoint.

KEY FACTS

| Parameter | Detail | Duration | 1990–2003 (13 years) | Cost | ~US$ 3 billion | Coordinating body |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| National Human Genome Research Institute | (NHGRI), USA + 18 countries | Director | James Watson (1990-1992); Francis Collins |  |  |  |
| (1993-2003) |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| | Private competition | Celera Genomics (Craig Venter) — completed | draft in 2001 | Publication | Draft genome published in Nature & Science, | February 2001; Complete sequence April 2003 | | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | | Human chromosomes | 46 (23 pairs) — ~3.2 billion base pairs | Number of genes | ~20,000-25,000 protein-coding genes (less than | expected) | Coding DNA | |  |  |  |  |
| TECHNOLOGIES USED |  |  |  |  |  |  |

- Sanger sequencing (chain termination method) — initial gold standard
- Shotgun sequencing — fragmenting genome and reassembling sequences
- Fluorescence-labeled dideoxy chain terminators
- Automated sequencing machines
- Bioinformatics tools for assembly and analysis

KEY FINDINGS

- Humans have approximately 20,000-25,000 genes (significantly fewer than the 100,000 predicted)
- 98.5% of DNA is non-coding ('junk DNA' — now known to have regulatory functions)
- 99.9% of DNA sequence is identical among all humans
- 0.1% variation accounts for individual differences in appearance, disease susceptibility
- Average gene contains ~27,000 base pairs
- Chromosome 1 has the most genes; Y chromosome the fewest

APPLICATIONS AND SIGNIFICANCE

1. MEDICINE AND DISEASE
- Identification of genes responsible for monogenic disorders (BRCA1/2, CFTR, HTT)
- Understanding complex disease genetics (CVD, diabetes, cancer, schizophrenia)
- Pharmacogenomics: tailoring drug doses to individual genetic profiles (precision medicine)
- Development of gene therapy approaches
- Companion diagnostics for targeted cancer therapies
2. DIAGNOSTICS
- Prenatal genetic testing (NIPT, chromosomal microarray)
- Newborn screening programs expanded
- Carrier testing for autosomal recessive conditions
- Genetic risk assessment for multifactorial diseases
3. BIOTECHNOLOGY
- Production of recombinant therapeutic proteins (insulin, erythropoietin, clotting factors)
- Development of monoclonal antibodies and biologic drugs
4. FORENSIC SCIENCE
- DNA fingerprinting for identification (Short Tandem Repeat — STR analysis)
- Paternity testing
5. EVOLUTIONARY BIOLOGY AND ANTHROPOLOGY
- Tracing human migration patterns (mitochondrial DNA analysis)
- Comparative genomics with other species

ETHICAL, LEGAL, AND SOCIAL ISSUES (ELSI)

- 15% of HGP funding allocated to ELSI research
- Genetic privacy and discrimination by employers/insurers
- Genetic Nondiscrimination Act (GINA), USA 2008
- Equitable access to genomic medicine
- Psychological impact of genetic risk information on patients
- Consent and ownership of genetic data
- Designer babies and enhancement via gene editing (CRISPR)

FUTURE: POST-GENOMIC ERA

- 1000 Genomes Project: cataloguing human genetic variation
171

- ENCODE Project: functional annotation of non-coding genome
- Pan-genome: capturing full human genetic diversity
- Next-generation sequencing (NGS): whole genome sequencing for <$1000 now possible
EXAM TIP: HGP: Key dates (1990-2003), key findings (20-25k genes, 1.5% coding), applications
(pharmacogenomics, gene therapy, forensics), ELSI. Mention Craig Venter/Celera competition for extra
credit.

Q1. Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs Theory ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ [Oct 2025 — NEW]
Abraham Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (1943, 'A Theory of Human Motivation') is a foundational
motivational theory in psychology and public health. It proposes that human behavior is driven by a
hierarchical set of needs, and that lower-order needs must be substantially satisfied before higher-

| order needs become motivating. | The Five-Level Hierarchy (Classical Model) | 184 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Level 1 — PHYSIOLOGICAL NEEDS (Deficiency Needs): Air, water, food, shelter, sleep, | warmth, clothing. These are survival needs and the most prepotent (powerful). Example: A | hungry patient cannot focus on health education. |
| 🧠 MNEMONIC — Maslow's 5 Levels — 'Please Send Love Each Saturday' | P — Physiological | S — Safety |
| Or: 'Pandas Sleep Like Enormous Snakes' | (bottom to top: Physiological → Safety → Love → Esteem → Self-Actualization) |  |

Q2. JOHARI Window — Describe and Its Applications ⭐⭐⭐ [May 2025 —

NEW]

The Johari Window is a psychological model developed by Joseph Luft and Harrington Ingham (1955)
— 'Jo-hari' from the first names of its creators. It is a framework for understanding interpersonal
communication, self-awareness, and group dynamics, with significant applications in health
communication and team management.
The Four Quadrants of Johari Window
Quadrant 1 — OPEN/ARENA (Known to self, known to others): Information freely shared.
Example: A doctor's published research, known clinical skills. This area should be
maximized for effective communication.
Quadrant 2 — BLIND SPOT (Unknown to self, known to others): Others see things about
you that you don't. Example: A clinician unaware of their dismissive communication style; a
patient's observable symptoms they haven't noticed. Feedback reduces this area.
Quadrant 3 — HIDDEN/FACADE (Known to self, unknown to others): Deliberately
concealed information. Example: A patient hiding substance use from their doctor. Self-
disclosure reduces this area.
Quadrant 4 — UNKNOWN (Unknown to self, unknown to others): Unexplored potential,
unconscious drives. Example: Latent clinical skills, undiscovered personality traits.

| Reflection and therapy unlock this. | Applications in Community Medicine & Public Health |
| --- | --- |
| Doctor-Patient Communication: Expanding the Open quadrant improves therapeutic alliance | and adherence |
| Community Needs Assessment: Unknown quadrant represents unrecognized community | health needs — needs participatory methods to uncover |
| Memory hook: 'Only Brilliant Hidden Unknowns grow with feedback' | Or: Jo-Hari = Joseph + Harrington (creators) |

subsections.content:

Q1. Iceberg Phenomenon and Its Application in Community Medicine

-

The Iceberg Phenomenon is one of the most fundamental concepts in epidemiology and community
medicine. Just as an iceberg has only a small visible portion above water with the vast bulk hidden
below the surface, most diseases in a community exist in a subclinical, undiagnosed, or unrecognized

| state — with only a fraction becoming clinically apparent. | Definition |
| --- | --- |
| The Iceberg Phenomenon states that for most diseases, clinical cases (visible above the 'waterline') | represent only the tip of the total disease burden, while a much larger proportion exists as subclinical |
| Prevalence: All existing cases at a time point — includes BOTH tip and visible part of | iceberg |
| Memory hook: 'Disease Drowns Deep Down' — 4 D's of the iceberg | Polio ratio: 1 paralytic : 200 subclinical = a very deep iceberg! |

Q2. Describe Iceberg Phenomenon — Differences Between Incidence and
Prevalence ⭐⭐⭐
(Core content covered above in Q1. Supplement with the following incidence-prevalence

| comparison:) | Incidence vs. Prevalence — Detailed Comparison |
| --- | --- |
| Incidence Rate: Number of NEW cases of disease occurring in a defined population during a |  |

specified time period / Population at risk × K. Measures: Risk of developing disease. Use:
Studying etiology and risk factors. Affected by: Duration of illness.
Prevalence Rate: Number of EXISTING cases (new + old) at a given point/period / Total
population × K. Measures: Disease burden and magnitude. Use: Health service planning.
Affected by: Incidence and duration.
Relationship: Prevalence ≈ Incidence × Mean Duration of Disease (for stable conditions)
192

In Iceberg context: Prevalence surveys reveal more of the iceberg than incidence reporting

| alone, as they capture long-standing undiagnosed cases | 🧠 MNEMONIC — Incidence vs Prevalence — 'NEW vs ALL' | Incidence = NEW cases only — measures RISK | Prevalence = ALL cases (new + old) — measures BURDEN |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| I = Incidence = 'I just got sick' (new) | P = Prevalence = 'Pool of all sick people' (all existing) |  |  |
| Formula link: P ≈ I × D (Prevalence ≈ Incidence × Duration) |  |  |  |

Q3. Implications of Iceberg Phenomenon on Planning Health Care Services

-

The iceberg phenomenon has profound implications for how health services are planned, funded,
and delivered. If health planners rely only on clinical/facility data (the visible tip), they will
systematically underestimate disease burden, misallocate resources, and fail to prevent disease

| progression in the subclinical majority. | Planning Implications — Detailed Analysis |

subsections.title: 26. CASE-CONTROL / COHORT / RCT STUDIES
subsections.content:
Q1. Steps in Conducting a Case-Control Study & Types of Biases ⭐⭐⭐⭐

Case-control studies are retrospective, observational studies that are particularly valuable for
studying rare diseases and multiple exposures. They are among the most efficient epidemiological
designs, forming the backbone of analytical epidemiology in community medicine.
Definition
A case-control study selects individuals with a disease (cases) and a suitable comparison group
without the disease (controls), then compares the frequency of exposure to putative risk factors in
both groups. The measure of association is the Odds Ratio (OR).
Steps in Conducting a Case-Control Study
Step 1 — Define the Research Question: Clear PICO framework (Population, Exposure,
Comparison, Outcome)
Step 2 — Select Cases: Define inclusion/exclusion criteria. Cases should be newly
diagnosed (incident cases preferred over prevalent to avoid survival bias). Sources: Hospital
records, disease registries, surveillance data.
Step 3 — Select Controls: Match to cases on potential confounders (age, sex,
socioeconomic status). Sources: Hospital controls, neighborhood controls, community
controls. Maintain 1:1 to 1:4 case:control ratio for efficiency.
194

Step 4 — Matching Strategy: Individual matching (pair each case to a control) or frequency
matching (match distributions). Over-matching can reduce statistical power — avoid
matching on variables in the causal pathway.
Step 5 — Measure Exposure: Structured questionnaire, medical records, biological
specimens. Exposure must be assessed for the relevant exposure period BEFORE disease
onset.
Step 6 — Minimize Bias: Use standardized data collection; train interviewers; blind
interviewers to case/control status where possible.
Step 7 — Calculate Odds Ratio (OR): 2×2 contingency table; OR = (a×d)/(b×c). OR >1:
positive association; OR <1: protective; OR = 1: no association.
Step 8 — Analyze Confounders: Stratified analysis (Mantel-Haenszel), logistic regression for
multiple confounders.
Step 9 — Interpret and Report: STROBE checklist for observational studies.

| Advantages | Efficient for rare diseases | Relatively inexpensive and quick | Can study multiple exposures simultaneously | No long follow-up required | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cannot calculate incidence or relative risk directly (only OR) | Subject to recall bias and selection bias | Poor for rare exposures | Temporal relationship (exposure → disease) difficult to establish | Types of Bias in Research Studies | SELECTION BIAS: Systematic error in choosing study participants. Types: Berkson's bias |


HAWTHORNE EFFECT: Participants behave differently when observed (affects
experimental studies).
DETECTION BIAS: Cases more intensively investigated, revealing associations
artefactually.
PUBLICATION BIAS: Positive results more likely to be published — inflates apparent effect

| sizes in meta-analyses. | Bias Reduction Strategies |
| --- | --- |
| Double-blinding, standardized questionnaires, multiple control groups, validation studies, | sensitivity analysis |
| Memory hook: 'Studies In Communities Have Problems Daily' — SICHPD | Key formula: OR = ad/bc (from 2×2 table) |

Q2. Issues and Implementation of Randomized Controlled Trial for a New
Vaccine ⭐⭐⭐
A Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT) is considered the gold standard for evaluating vaccine efficacy
because randomization controls for both known and unknown confounders. However, conducting an
RCT for a new vaccine involves complex logistical, ethical, and statistical challenges.
Definition
An RCT is a prospective, experimental study design in which participants are randomly allocated to
receive either the intervention (vaccine) or a control (placebo or comparator vaccine), and outcomes

| are compared between groups. | Phases of Vaccine Development |
| --- | --- |
| Phase I: Safety in healthy volunteers (20-100 participants). Dose-finding, immunogenicity | testing. |
| Phase III: Large-scale efficacy RCT (thousands of participants). Measures vaccine efficacy | (VE) in real-world-like conditions. |
| I — ITT Analysis | D — DSMB monitoring |
| Memory hook: 'Please Run Studies Ethically — Best For India's Diseases Always' |  |

Q3. Critically Appraise Randomized Controlled Trial ⭐⭐⭐
Critical appraisal of an RCT involves systematically evaluating the validity, results, and applicability
of a trial. The CONSORT (Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials) checklist and CASP (Critical
Appraisal Skills Programme) RCT checklist are international standards used for this purpose.
Framework for Critical Appraisal — Three Questions
A) Are the results VALID? (Internal validity — minimize bias)
B) What are the RESULTS? (Effect size, precision)
C) Are the results APPLICABLE? (External validity — generalizability)
A) Validity Assessment
Was randomization appropriate? — Check: Sequence generation (computer random
numbers, lottery); Allocation concealment (sealed envelopes, central allocation); Balance of
baseline characteristics between groups.
Were all participants accounted for? — <20% dropout acceptable; Intention-to-treat (ITT)
analysis performed?
Was blinding maintained? — Double-blind preferred; Outcome assessor blinding minimum
Were groups treated equally? — Co-interventions, contamination, compliance assessed?
Was the follow-up adequate? — Duration sufficient to detect meaningful outcomes?
B) Results Assessment
Effect Size: Relative Risk (RR), Odds Ratio (OR), Number Needed to Treat (NNT), Vaccine
Efficacy (VE)
Precision: 95% Confidence Intervals — narrow CIs indicate more precise estimates
Statistical Significance: p-value <0.05 conventional threshold, but CI more informative
Clinical Significance vs. Statistical Significance: Small but statistically significant effects may
lack clinical importance
NNT = 1 / Absolute Risk Reduction — lower NNT means more beneficial treatment
C) Applicability Assessment
Are my patients similar to trial participants? (generalizability)
198

| Was the intervention feasible in my setting? | Were all clinically important outcomes measured? | Are benefits worth harms and costs? | CONSORT Checklist Highlights | Title and abstract: RCT clearly identified | PICO clearly defined |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Sample size justification provided | Randomization and allocation concealment described | Blinding status and limitations stated | Flow diagram (CONSORT flow chart) showing participant progress through trial | ITT analysis performed and stated | 🧠 MNEMONIC — RCT Appraisal — 'VRA' Framework |


  For Validity, remember 'RABBIT':
  R — Randomization proper?
  A — Allocation concealment?
  B — Blinding maintained?
  B — Baseline characteristics balanced?
  I — ITT analysis done?
  T — Trial completion adequate?

Q4. Enumerate Various Biases in Research. Explain Influence & How to
Reduce ⭐⭐⭐⭐ [Apr/May 2026 — NEW]
Bias is defined as any systematic error in the design, conduct, analysis, or reporting of a study that
results in a mistaken estimate of an exposure's effect on disease risk. Unlike random error (reduced
by increasing sample size), bias cannot be corrected by statistical methods alone — it must be

| prevented during study design. | Classification of Biases | I. SELECTION BIAS — Error in how participants are enrolled |
| --- | --- | --- |
| a. Berkson's Bias: Hospital controls are sicker than the general population, leading to | spurious associations | 199 |
| b. Healthy Worker Effect: Employed workers are healthier than general population — | underestimates occupational hazards | c. Volunteer Bias: Volunteers tend to be healthier and more health-conscious than non- |
| IV. OTHER BIASES |  |  |
| Hawthorne Effect: Subjects change behavior when observed | Publication Bias: Positive studies preferentially published — inflates systematic review | estimates |
| Allocation Bias: Non-random treatment allocation in non-RCT studies | How Biases Influence Study Results | Selection Bias → Spurious associations or masking of real associations |
| Memory hook: Studies In Communities HELP reduce bias |  |  |

  To REDUCE bias remember 'RAMP':
  R — Randomize
  A — Allocate concealed
  M — Measure blindly
  P — Pre-register protocol

Q1. Briefly Explain Measures of Central Tendency with Example (2018) ★★★★

INTRODUCTION

Measures of central tendency are statistical values that describe the center or typical value of a dataset.
They summarize a large set of data into a single representative figure, facilitating comparison and
interpretation. The three principal measures are Mean, Median, and Mode.

1. ARITHMETIC MEAN (Average)
The arithmetic mean is the sum of all observations divided by the total number of observations.
Formula: Mean (X̄ ) = Sum of all values (ΣX) / Number of observations (N)

| Example: Haemoglobin levels (g/dL): 10, 11, 12, 13, 14 | Mean = (10+11+12+13+14)/5 = 60/5 = 12 g/dL |
| --- | --- |
| Best used for: Normally distributed continuous data (height, weight, BP) | Advantage: Uses all observations; mathematically tractable |
| 2. MEDIAN | The median is the middle value when observations are arranged in ascending or descending order. It |
| | divides the distribution into two equal halves. | Calculation: | Odd N: Median = value of [(N+1)/2]th observation | | | --- | --- | --- | |
| 3. MODE | The mode is the value that occurs most frequently in a dataset. |
| 203 |  |
| RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN MEAN, MEDIAN & MODE |  |
| Distribution Type | Relationship |
| CHOICE OF MEASURE — PEARL TABLE |  |
| Situation | Best Measure |
| MNEMONIC |  |
| "M-M-M — Mean Minds the Middle, Median Manages Skew, | Mode Maps the Most" |

Q2. Explain Different Measures of Central Tendency with Examples, Merits and
Demerits (2021) ★★★
(This is a combined question — see Q1 above for Central Tendency. Below covers Variability in detail)

Q3. Explain Different Measures of Variability with Examples (2020) ★★★

INTRODUCTION

Measures of variability (dispersion) describe the spread or scatter of data around the central value. They
complement central tendency measures by showing how homogeneous or heterogeneous a dataset is.
The principal measures are: Range, Mean Deviation, Standard Deviation, Variance, Coefficient of
Variation, and Percentiles/Quartiles.

| 1. RANGE | Range = Maximum value − Minimum value |
| --- | --- |
| Example: BP readings 110, 120, 130, 140, 160 mmHg → Range = 160 − 110 = 50 mmHg | Merit: Simplest; quick estimate of spread |
| 2. MEAN DEVIATION (Average Deviation) | Mean Deviation = Σ|X − X̄ | / N (sum of absolute deviations from mean divided by N) |
| 3. STANDARD DEVIATION (SD) — MOST IMPORTANT ★ | SD is the square root of the average of squared deviations from the mean. It is the most widely used |

| Properties of Normal Distribution using SD: | Mean ± 1 SD → covers 68.27% of observations |
| --- | --- |
| | Mean ± 2 SD → covers 95.45% of observations | Mean ± 3 SD → covers 99.73% of observations | Example: Mean haemoglobin = 12 g/dL, SD = 1.5 g/dL | | | --- | --- | --- | |
| 4. VARIANCE | Variance = SD² = Σ(X − X̄ )² / N |
| Used in ANOVA and regression analysis | Demerit: Units are squared (e.g., g²/dL²), hence less interpretable directly |
| 5. COEFFICIENT OF VARIATION (CV) | CV = (SD / Mean) × 100%  — a dimensionless measure, useful for comparing variability between groups |
| 6. PERCENTILES AND QUARTILES | Percentile: Value below which a given percentage of observations fall |

Q1 (25th percentile), Q2 (50th = Median), Q3 (75th percentile)
Inter-Quartile Range (IQR) = Q3 − Q1: Most robust measure; used in Box plots; not affected by
extremes
Public Health Application: Growth charts use 3rd, 10th, 50th, 90th, 97th percentiles for child
nutrition assessment (WHO/IAP standards).

MNEMONIC

| --- | --- |
| R = Range | V = Variance | SD = Standard Deviation (MOST IMPORTANT) | | C = Coefficient of Variation (unit-free, comparative) | P = Percentiles/IQR |

Q1. Medical Sociology — Definition and Significance (May 2025) ★★★★ *NEW*

DEFINITION

Medical Sociology is the branch of sociology concerned with the study of social factors, social
relationships, social structures, and social institutions as they relate to health, illness, medical care, and
the healthcare system. It examines how society shapes and is shaped by health phenomena.
The term was first coined by Charles McIntire (1894). It gained academic recognition in the 1950s–60s
through the works of Talcott Parsons ('The Social System', 1951) and Robert Straus.

SCOPE OF MEDICAL SOCIOLOGY

Sociology OF medicine: Studies doctors, nurses, hospitals, doctor-patient relationships as social
phenomena
Sociology IN medicine: Applies sociological methods to solve medical/public health problems
Social determinants of health: How class, caste, gender, and poverty influence health outcomes
Health behaviour: Why people seek or avoid healthcare (Illness Behaviour Model, Health Belief
Model)
Medicalization of social problems: Defining social issues (alcoholism, obesity) as medical
conditions

SIGNIFICANCE OF MEDICAL SOCIOLOGY IN PUBLIC HEALTH

1. Understanding disease distribution: Social stratification explains differential morbidity/mortality
patterns across SES groups
2. Doctor-patient relationship: Sociological analysis improves communication and adherence
3. Health behavior change: Identifies social barriers to healthcare utilization and compliance
4. Healthcare organization: Helps design effective hospital structures, referral systems, team
dynamics
5. Planning preventive services: Social diagnosis is integral to community health programmes
(PRECEDE-PROCEED model)
6. Cultural competence: Understanding beliefs, stigma, and taboos improves community
acceptance of health interventions
7. Policy formulation: Identifies social determinants requiring intersectoral action (e.g., NHP 2017,
SDGs)

TALCOTT PARSONS' SICK ROLE THEORY

Q2. Role of Culture and Social Structure in Health and Disease (Dec 2024) ★★★

*NEW*

SOCIAL STRUCTURE — DEFINITION

Social structure refers to the organized pattern of social relationships and social institutions that together
constitute society. Key components include social class, caste, gender, family structure, religion, and
occupational roles.

ROLE OF SOCIAL STRUCTURE IN HEALTH

1. Social Class and Health
The Black Report (UK, 1980) and WHO Commission on SDH (2008) document clear inverse relationships
between social class and health outcomes:
Lower socioeconomic class → higher infant mortality, malnutrition, communicable diseases,
mental illness
Higher class → better access to healthcare, nutrition, and health information
In India: SC/ST populations carry disproportionate burden of TB, malnutrition, and maternal
mortality

2. Family Structure
Nuclear family: Reduced caregiver support for elderly, children; isolation; mental health stress
Joint family: Better social support; shared childcare; but higher communicable disease
transmission
Single-parent families: Higher poverty rates, poorer child health outcomes

3. Gender and Health
Women: Higher morbidity but lower mortality (female survival advantage)
Patriarchal structures: Delayed healthcare for women; undernutrition; poor reproductive health
Men: Higher risk-taking behaviour; higher mortality from injuries, CVD, substance abuse

ROLE OF CULTURE IN HEALTH AND DISEASE

1. Cultural Beliefs and Healthcare Seeking
Illness explanatory models: Supernatural causation → faith healers preferred over modern
medicine
Stigma: Mental illness, HIV/AIDS, TB — cultural stigma delays diagnosis and treatment
Traditional medicine: Ayurveda, Unani, home remedies — can complement or delay allopathic
care

2. Dietary Practices
Religious food taboos: Vegetarianism, fasting → nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12)
Cultural preferences: High salt diet (hypertension), excess ghee/sweets (diabetes/CVD)
Colostrum discarding, late introduction of complementary foods → malnutrition in infants

| Adolescent pregnancy, maternal mortality | Purdah/seclusion | Vitamin D deficiency, osteomalacia | Sickle cell (certain tribes) | Protective against malaria but causes disease | Tobacco/pan chewing |
